questions.text: I hold the view that treating another's suffering as morally significant is a non-negotiable part of how I want to live.
I notice that waiting for important outcomes produces alertness in me, not agitation.
I find that my approach to emotionally difficult problems is most effective when it begins with questions, not feelings.
I notice that my emotional states leave a kind of afterimage that takes time to clear.
I believe that building wealth and security for myself is a worthy goal.
I find that having a detailed personal five-year plan gives me clarity and drive.
I think that the value of emotional composure during crises is best understood in terms of the outcomes it enables.
I trust that I can handle whatever comes up, so I don't waste time on contingency plans.
I tend to feel guilty when I spend a whole day on my own interests.
I put things wherever there's space and rely on my memory to find them.
I would rather live simply so I can contribute more to the people and causes around me.
I notice that my body stays relaxed in situations that make other people tense.
I find that the moral demand of suffering is the consideration that most reliably prevents me from becoming morally complacent.
I pick up on when a person quotes someone else to lend authority to an opinion they are not confident enough to own.
I routinely take on administrative and logistical work so that others can focus on what they do best.
I believe that the most helpful stance during someone's crisis is one of calm assessment rather than emotional immersion.
I wait to be invited into other people's initiatives.
I do not naturally decode the layers of meaning in polite conversation.
I rarely notice shifts in someone's vocal tone during a conversation.
I find that advance planning allows me to be more present during execution.
I am typically the one who breaks a tie in a group vote.
I find that I can be disappointed without the disappointment coloring the rest of my day.
I find that competing with others for top positions energizes me.
I feel that once I commit to a direction, others should follow.
My passions tend to consume me rather than simmer in the background.
I feel that being willing to abandon a failing approach is more valuable than consistency.
I am the kind of person who reads the manual before using a new device.
I find that the moral pull of suffering is the force that has most powerfully connected my daily actions to my deepest understanding of right and wrong.
I have a tendency to take over tasks that others are doing too slowly.
I can tell when a couple is mid-argument even though they are being perfectly civil in public.
I sense the unspoken ranking of whose time is considered most valuable in a professional setting.
I do not usually sense when the mood of a text conversation shifts because of an emoji choice or its absence.
I feel most in my element when the go or no-go call is a genuine group decision.
I am the type of person who always has a five-year plan or long-range vision.
My gut reacts to emotional information before my conscious mind has processed it.
I am energized by arriving at a decision that everyone shaped.
